title: All-butter shortbread rounds
subtitle: Classic Scottish shortbread with a crumbly snap
excerpt: Shortbread uses a 1:2:3 ratio of sugar to butter to flour. The high butter content makes the dough tender and prone to crumbling if overworked, mix just enough to bring it together, then press rather than roll if needed. The biscuits should be pale gold, not browned.
sourceNotes: Shortbread is a Scottish biscuit documented in domestic cookbooks from at least the sixteenth century, though it became widely standardised in the nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, shortbread appears in the biscuits chapter with the 1:2:3 sugar-butter-flour formula identified as the classic ratio.

content.text: The crumbly texture of shortbread comes from the

effect of the high butter content, the fat prevents gluten from developing, which keeps the biscuit tender. Mix the dough only until it just comes together, and chill it before rolling if it feels too soft to handle.
Make the dough and chill, 30 minutes

Rub {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} and {{salt-table}} with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs, some pea-sized butter pieces are fine. Stir in {{caster-sugar}}. Squeeze the mixture together until it forms a dough; it will be crumbly but should hold when pressed. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 150°C (130°C fan). Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to about 7 mm thick, thicker than you think, shortbread shrinks to almost nothing in thickness. Cut into rounds with a 6 to 7 cm cutter and place on lined baking trays. Prick each biscuit three or four times with a fork to the base. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes until set and very pale gold at the edges; the centres should look barely cooked. Transfer to a rack and dust with caster sugar while warm. Leave to cool completely; they firm as they cool.
